role_summary: Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas community organizer; mother of nine; collaborator with SNCC fieldworkers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137) in 1960s Arkansas civil rights organizing; later Head Start program worker and founder of a Marvell community center.
ai_reading: Newsome Jackson's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 157; the broader cluster includes Myrtle Gonza Glascoe (entry 137, the SNCC field secretary she collaborated with in Phillips County), Rosie Head (entry 111), and James Oscar Jones (entry 57). The Glascoe pairing is the catalog's clearest local-community-organizer-and-SNCC-field-secretary collaboration cosine: Newsome Jackson was the resident community partner who hosted Glascoe and Himmelbaum in their Phillips County voter-registration work, and the embedding catches that local-partner register through both their testimonies. The novel observation her page surfaces is the maternal-organizer-with-nine-children signature: Newsome Jackson's nine children meant her civil rights organizing happened from a position of substantial domestic responsibility, the catalog's clearest case of a maternal-community-anchor organizing role.
biographical_paragraph: While Newsome Jackson's LoC oral history carries an ingestion-only audit tier (the catalog ingested the transcript but did not subject it to the audit cascade, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 136 and the Civil Rights Digital Library [src: 1, src: 2]. Newsome Jackson was born in 1923 in Madison, Illinois; she married Earliss Jackson in 1943 and they had nine children. She settled in Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas, where she became a community organizer; her early local-organizing work included rallying her community to renovate a segregated school. She assisted SNCC field secretaries Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137) in their Phillips County 1960s voter-registration and community-organizing work [src: 2]. After the most-photographed civil rights years she worked for the federal Head Start program and helped found a Marvell community center, the institutional legacy of her decades of local organizing [src: 2].
